contents: ArticleIncreased Pulse Wave Velocity in SystemicLupus Erythematosus: A Meta-AnalysisPeng Wang, MM1,2, Yan-Mei Mao, MM1,2, Chan-Na Zhao, MM1,2,Li-Na Liu, MM1,2, Xiao-Mei Li, BM3, Xiang-Pei Li, BM3,and Hai-Feng Pan, MD1,2AbstractSystemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is associated with increased cardiovascular risk. This study aims to derive a more preciseestimation on pulse wave velocity (PWV) level in patients with SLE and related factors. A literature search was performed usingPubMed, EMBASE, and The Cochrane Library databases, studies published up to February 28, 2017, in English. Pooled stan-dardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence interval (CI) was calculated by fixed-effect or random-effect model analysis.Heterogeneity test was performed by the Q statistic and quantified using I2; publication bias was evaluated using a funnel plot andEgger's linear regression test. Of 156 studies found, 27 met eligibility criteria, and 14 studies were finally included in the meta-analysis. Meta-analysis revealed that the SLE group had significantly higher PWV levels than the control group; SMD  1/4 0.56 and95% CI (0.30-0.82). Subgroup analyses showed that body mass index (BMI), sample size, and disease duration were associated withPWV in patients with SLE. Overall, our study suggests that patients with SLE have a higher PWV level, and it is associated with BMI,sample size, and disease duration.

Keywordssystemic lupus erythematosus, pulse wave velocity, meta-analysisIntroductionSystemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is one of the commonestconnective tissue diseases that span a wide range of clinical fea-tures, including accelerated atherosclerosis that have a 4- to 10-fold increased risk of developing cardiovascular (CV) diseases(CVD) compared with the general population.1-4 Possible expla-nations for accelerated atherosclerosis in SLE may be due to ahigh prevalence of conventional risk factors,5,6 long-term corti-costeroid use,7 the presence of antiphospholipid antibodies,8 andproatherogenic pathophysiologic phenomena inherent to SLE, allofwhichleadachroniclow-grade inflammatorystate,endothelialdysfunction, and eventually atherosclerotic events.9Arterial stiffness, recognized as a marker of early vascularaging (EVA), can reflect changes in mechanical wall propertiesthat occur early at onset of vascular disease and predispose tomajor CVD.10,11 Moreover, increased arterial stiffness has beenrecognized as an independent risk factor for CVD and measur-ing it could become an important routine assessment of patientsin daily cardiology practice.12 Arterial stiffness is commonlyassessed by metrics of pulse wave velocity (PWV). Pulse wavevelocity is defined as the arterial pulse's velocity of movingalong the vessel wall and is an indicator of arterial distensibil-ity.13 Measured PWV is an indicator of arterial wall stiffnessand also inversely proportional to arterial dilatation capacity orarterial compliance. Pulse wave velocity is not only a measureof arterial stiffness but also a strong predictor of CV events and

all-cause mortality.14,15 Although PWV levels have beenassessed in a number of studies in patients with SLE,16-19 theresults are still not consistent. The present study aimed to deter-mine, through a systematic review and meta-analysis of pub-lished reports, a more precise estimation of PWV levels inpatients with SLE. In addition, we also explored the relatedinfluencing factors of PWV levels in SLE.MethodsSearch StrategyWe performed a prespecified search strategy to identify whenPWV first appeared in the literature, including relevant studiesin the following databases: PubMed, Embase, and TheCochrane Library database. The keywords were used in all1 Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics, School of Public Health, AnhuiMedical University, Hefei, Anhui, China2 The Key Laboratory of Major Autoimmune Diseases, Anhui Province, Hefei,Anhui, China3 Department of Rheumatology, Anhui Provincial Hospital, Hefei, Anhui, ChinaCorresponding Author:Hai-Feng Pan, Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics, School of PublicHealth, Anhui Medical University, 81 Meishan Road, Hefei, Anhui 230032,China.Email: Angiology1-8a The Author(s) 2017Reprints and permission:sagepub.com/journalsPermissions.navDOI: 10.1177/0003319717715964journals.sagepub.com/home/angfields: "systemic lupus erythematosus" OR "SLE" OR "lupus"AND "carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity" OR "cf-PWV" OR"pulse wave velocity" OR "PWV" OR "aortic PWV" OR"aPWV." All keywords variants were used to increase thenumber of studies returned by the search. No method or lan-guage restrictions were applied, and studies from all countrieswere eligible. The references in the identified or related articleswere manually scanned for other published articles. All result-ing articles were then screened for PWV measures and theinclusion of healthy control groups.Inclusion Criteria and Exclusion Criteria

For inclusion in the meta-analysis, all the following criteria hadto be met: (1) case-control, cohort, or cross-sectional studieswith data on both patients diagnosed as having SLE and healthyage- and sex-matched controls and (2) provide detailed dataabout PWV level in both patients with SLE and healthy con-trols. All patients with SLE had to fulfill the standard accepteddiagnostic criteria and healthy controls needed to be clearlydescribed so as to be included in our study. If any study wasreported in more than 1 publication, we considered all publi-cations for data abstraction, but only 1 was included in ourstudy. Patients with a history of coronary diseases, infectiousdiseases, neoplasia, kidney or liver insufficiency, or symptomssuggestive of CVD (angina, arrhythmia, congestive heart fail-ure, stroke, and peripheral arterial disease) were excluded. Theexclusion criteria are shown in further detail in Figure 1.Data ExtractionThe quality of the included studies was independently assessedand scored by 2 investigators (Peng Wang and Yan-Mei Mao)using the Newcastle-Ottawa quality assessment scale (NOS)

for observational studies.20 In the case of disagreement, a thirdinvestigator (Li-Na Liu) resolved the disagreement. The meanPWV values (standard deviation) and sample size wereextracted, independently with the standard protocol from allarticles comparing the PWV of patients with SLE with thatof controls. If original important data were unavailable in rel-evant articles, we e-mailed the corresponding author to obtainthe further details.Statistical AnalysisWe extracted the following data from each study, includingprimary author's name, year of publication, country, samplesize of 2 groups, the mean and standard deviation of PWV bothin SLE and controls, and other related data. The mean PWVdifference (95% confidence interval [CI]) between patientswith SLE and control subjects was calculated by using thestandardized mean difference (SMD), and its 95% CI wasdescribed by a forest plot. The data distribution was assumedto be normal with a z value of th0.68 corresponding to thereported 25th and 75th percentiles. In this manner, the standarddeviation was calculated.21 Statistical heterogeneity withinstudies was examined by the Q statistic.22 Then, we estimatedthe effect of heterogeneity in each study by the method, I2  1/4[(Q  df)/Q]  100%.23 I2 values of 25%, 50%, and 75% werequalitatively classified as low, moderate, and high, respec-tively. If there was no statistical difference about heterogeneity(P > .05) among these studies, the fixed-effect model was used.Otherwise, a random-effect model was used for further analy-ses. Publication bias was visually estimated by a funnel plot,and Egger's regression asymmetry test was performed toexplore asymmetry of the funnel plot.24 If bias was found, theadditional tests were applied. To detect the sources of hetero-geneity, further subgroup analysis was performed according tobody mass index (BMI), sample size, and disease duration. Allstatistical analyses were conducted by Stata 12.0 software.ResultsStudy Characteristics

We identified 156 studies by using electronic and manualsearch methods. Due to duplicate publication, unclear indica-tors, unmatched comparisons, review, and conferenceabstracts, 129 articles were excluded. Of the other 27 potentialarticles, 14 were finally incorporated in the meta-analysisaccording to the mentioned criteria. A total of 1752 participants(854 patients with SLE and 898 healthy controls) were ulti-mately included. The basic characteristics of the selected stud-ies are summarized in Table 1.16-19,25-34 In 9 studies, patientswere diagnosed with SLE by the American College of Rheu-matology (ACR) classification criteria, whereas the other 5studies did not mention the diagnostic criteria. The age, sex,and BMI were matched between patients with SLE and controlsin each included study. Newcastle-Ottawa quality assessmentscale were determined to assess the quality of included studies(Table 1). Most studies provided high quality as a whole, inwhich the scores of included studies ranged from 5 to 8 stars.Heterogeneity Test ResultsStatistical heterogeneity among the selected studies wasassessed with the w2 heterogeneity statistic Q and then quanti-fied with I2, which measures the inconsistency across studyresults and describes the proportion of total variation in studyestimates that is the result of heterogeneity rather than chance.In this study, the results showed that there was marked hetero-geneity among the studies (I2  1/4 84.50%, P < .001; Figure 2).

Overall Effects and Subgroup AnalysisOverall, through a random-effect model, compared withhealthy controls, the analysis showed that patients with SLEhad significantly higher PWV (SMD  1/4 0.56, 95% CI: 0.30-0.82; Figure 2). Subgroup analyses showed that BMI, samplesize, and disease duration were positively associated with PWVin SLE (Table 2).Sensitivity Analyses and Publication BiasA sensitivity analysis compared the meta-analysis results of thestudies as a whole to the same meta-analysis with 1 studyexcluded in each round to determine how robust the findingswere. In our study, the results showed no significant changewhen any 1 study was excluded using random-effect methods(Figure 3). Egger's linear regression test was applied to assessthe asymmetry of the funnel plot,35 and no significant publica-tion bias was found as a whole (P > .05; Figure 4).

DiscussionCardiovascular diseases is a major cause of morbidity andmortality in SLE.1 Arterial stiffness is a consequence of arterialwall thickening and early changes in mechanical properties ofthe vascular wall.36 Previous studies have recognized the linkbetween aortic stiffness and CV events.12 Aortic PWV is notonly a measure of arterial stiffness but also a strong predictorfor the future CV events.37 Furthermore, an abnormal PWV isassociated with an increased risk of CVD.38Several studies have evaluated arterial stiffness in patientswith SLE. In 41 patients with SLE, arterial stiffness wasincreased despite a low risk for CVD according to the Framing-ham score,18 the study of Bjarnegrad et al39 in 27 patients withSLE and 27 controls demonstrated that carotid-femoral pulsewave velocity (cf-PWV) was higher in patients with SLE thanin controls after correcting for mean arterial pressure and BMI.In addition, the study of Yildiz et al34 found higher aortic PWVin 24 premenopausal women with SLE compared with 24 age-and sex-matched controls. However, both the study byTziomalos et al16 (including 55 patients with SLE and 61controls) and the study by Quinlan et al30 (including 45 patientswith SLE and 40 controls) found that PWV did not differbetween patients with SLE and controls.

In our study, we reviewed 14 comparisons of populationswith SLE disease populations (n  1/4 854) versus controls (n  1/4898). Meta-analysis revealed that there is a statistically signif-icantly greater PWV in patients with SLE compared with con-trols, supporting the evidence for an increased CVD burden inpatients with SLE. Heterogeneity tests showed marked hetero-geneity among the studies. Heterogeneity refers to the degreeof difference in results among the included studies. Strongheterogeneity suggests that the validity of the meta-analysisis impaired. Egger's tests suggested no publication bias. Tominimize the potential influential factor for the results, andto explore the source of heterogeneity, we performed the sub-group analysis. By evaluating the BMI, sample size, and dis-ease duration as subgroups, we found that there weresignificantly higher PWV levels in different subgroup ofpatients with SLE, but BMI 25 group (SMD  1/4 0.37, 95%CI: 0.19 to 0.93), which may be attributed to different treat-ment regimen and clinical heterogeneity among patients withSLE. For patients with SLE, disease duration 10 years group,BMI <25, and sample size (n  60) groups, each group indi-vidually had higher PWV when compared with controls in thesubgroup analysis. This suggested that BMI, sample size, anddisease duration may be correlated with the levels of PWV inpatients with SLE. Nevertheless, due to different clinical char-acteristics, sex ratio, and race differences, we could not explorethe other possible positive relationships. The potential mechan-ism of increased CV burden in patients with SLE may beattributed to chronic inflammatory state, where C-reactive pro-tein (CRP) and a number of circulating and endothelium-derived factors, including nitric oxide, von Willebrand factor(vWF), excess of adhesive molecules, and endothelin-1, mayinfluence large artery elasticity providing functional regulationof arterial stiffness.40-42

There are several limitations in the current study. First, someof the studies did not have a systemic lupus erythematosusdisease activity index (SLEDAI) reported, so we could notdirectly address aspects of the relationship between PWV andSLEDAI. Second, some studies had small samples from a clin-ical population or included individuals from only 1 ethnic pop-ulation and are thus limited in their generalizability. Third,the partial data were approximation numbers; thus, thismay be biased compared with the actuality. Fourth, thisAuthor,YearRegionPatients With SLEControlAorticPWVMethodCriteria for theClassificationof SLEStudy Type NOSnAge (Mean[SD]),Years%FemaleDuration,YearsSLEDAI(Means)nAge (Mean[SD]),Years%FemaleAbbreviations: ACR, American College of Rheumatology; cf-pwv, carotid to femoral pulse wave velocity; NA, not available; NOS, Newcastle-Ottawa Scale;SD, standard deviation; SLE, systemic lupus erythematosus; SLEDAI, SLE disease activity index; PWV, pulse wave velocity.

Stratification GroupnSMD (95% CI)Heterogeneity TestPublication BiasQPI2, %TPAbbreviations: BMI, body mass index; CI, confidence interval; PWV, pulse wave velocity; SLE, systemic lupus erythematosus; SMD, standardized mean difference.meta-analysis used a random-effect model which may lead to amore conservative conclusion. Furthermore, this meta-analysisis a retrospective study and could not prove causation which issusceptible to the methodological deficiencies of the pooledstudies.There are, however, particular strengths. Compared withsingle-country studies, we enhanced generalizability by com-bining findings from 14 studies across 3 continents. Subgroupanalysis was conducted to control the heterogeneity in ourstudy. In addition, no publication bias was detected indicatingthat the included results may be unbiased. Finally, sensitivityanalyses showed that our results were robust.In summary, we conclude that PWV are higher in patientswith SLE compared with healthy controls. In addition, wefound that the PWV was influenced by BMI, sample size, anddisease duration. However, further analyses with larger sam-ples are still needed to determine the precise relationshipsbetween SLE and PWV.Declaration of Conflicting InterestsThe author(s) declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect tothe research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.
